metadata.title: How Anthropic Actually Builds Products with Claude.

skin_warnings: B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'LogoOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam for Humanitarians AI.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · How Anthropic Actually Builds Products with Claude.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Phase one is prompts only: Claude interviews you and produces a spec you evaluate, not a prototype. Phase two delivers four divergent HTML mockups, each a cheap bet on how the interface should feel. Phase three specifies components with machine-readable DOM contracts a test runner verifies. Running the verifier makes the AI output checkable without anyone reading the code itself.

artifactLines: Phase one is prompts only: Claude interviews you and produces a spec you evaluate, not a prototype.
Phase two delivers four divergent HTML mockups, each a cheap bet on how the interface should feel.
Phase three specifies components with machine-readable DOM contracts a test runner verifies.
Running the verifier makes the AI output checkable without anyone reading the code itself.

beats.narration_text: Here is why How Anthropic Actually Builds Products with Claude. belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: Phase one is prompts only: Claude interviews you and produces a spec you evaluate, not a prototype.
Phase two delivers four divergent HTML mockups, each a cheap bet on how the interface should feel.
Phase three specifies components with machine-readable DOM contracts a test runner verifies.
Running the verifier makes the AI output checkable without anyone reading the code itself.

beats.narration_text: What grabs me here is that phase three makes AI output checkable without reading the code — DOM contracts as machine-readable invariants a test runner verifies. I keep shipping code I can't fully audit, so I want to build that verification surface. Can you (1) take a simple login-form component and write two DOM-contract invariants for it; (2) explain how bun run verify would report a pass versus a fail on each; (3) tell me which architectural gaps this catches that a normal code review would miss?

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · BUILD WITH CLAUDE · ANTHROPIC
props.segment: How Anthropic Actually Builds Products with Claude.
props.command: What grabs me here is that phase three makes AI output checkable without reading the code — DOM contracts as machine-readable invariants a test runner verifies. I keep shipping code I can't fully audit, so I want to build that verification surface. Can you (1) take a simple login-form component and write two DOM-contract invariants for it; (2) explain how bun run verify would report a pass versus a fail on each; (3) tell me which architectural gaps this catches that a normal code review would miss?

beats.narration_text: Phase one is prompts only. Claude interviews you as a potential user, synthesizes the responses into a product brief, and produces a verifiable requirements list. No code yet. The output is a spec you can evaluate — not a prototype you have to run.

graphic.production_viz: Three-column pipeline: Interview → Brief → Requirements. Phase 1 column highlights.

beats.narration_text: Phase two produces four divergent design directions — static HTML mockups — each with a different visual philosophy. Flat minimal, card-based, dashboard, mobile-first. You pick one or synthesize across them. The mockups are not final UI — they are cheap bets on what the interface should feel like.

graphic.production_viz: 2x2 grid of labeled design direction boxes — each with a style label

beats.narration_text: Phase three is the surprising one. Components are specified with DOM contracts: machine-readable invariants that a test runner verifies. Run 'bun run verify' and each component either passes or fails its spec. This makes the AI output checkable without reading the code — the verification surface is what you audit.

graphic.production_viz: Verification table: component names, their DOM contracts, PASS/FAIL status column

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude: 'Write a DOM contract for a bill-splitting component — list the invariants a test runner could verify.' That question forces you to specify behavior before implementing it — which is the whole point of phase three. What does a verifiable component actually look like? Run it. Liam, in for Bear.
